# Guide {-}
SmartPunctuation
Go to iPad setting> General > Keyboard and toggle off Smart Punctuation. If you don't,
the iPad will turn your straight code quotes "" into curly "smart" quotes “”, which will throw errors in
Browser Choice: Safari works well, but if you encounter "session disconnected" issues, try Google Chrome on the iPad;
it often handles the RStudio server connection more stably.
A Note on Dro   Posit Cloud does not "sync" with Dropbox like your Mac does. Any changes you make on your iPad will stay in the cloud.
When you get back to your Mac, you'll need to export the files back out of Posit Cloud and drop them
into your Dropbox folder to keep everything aligned.

Would you like me to show you how to connect your Posit Cloud project
to GitHub so your Mac and iPad can stay synced automatically?
Using GitHub is the "pro" way to do this. Instead of manually zipping and
unzipping files like it’s 2005, GitHub acts as the middleman.
You "push" changes from your Mac to GitHub, and then "pull" them down onto Posit
Cloud (and vice versa).

Here is how to set up that bridge.

Phase 1: Push your Mac Project to GitHub
Before touching Posit Cloud, your code needs to live in a GitHub repository (repo).

Open your project in RStudio on your Mac.

Initialize Git: Go to Tools > Project Options... > Git/SVN. Change the "Version control system" to Git.
RStudio will ask to restart.

Connect to GitHub: * Open your terminal (inside RStudio) and type:
usethis::use_github()

(Note: This requires the usethis package. If you don't have a Personal Access Token set up, it will guide you through creating one.)

Once finished, your code is now safely on GitHub.

Phase 2: Connect Posit Cloud to GitHub
Now we tell Posit Cloud to go grab that specific repo.

Log in to Posit Cloud.

Click New Project > New Project from Git Repository.

Paste the URL of your GitHub repo (e.g., https://github.com/yourname/folder2).

Authentication: Posit Cloud will likely ask for your GitHub credentials.
Use your GitHub username and your Personal Access Token (PAT) as the password.

Phase 3: The "Holiday" Workflow (iPad)
When you are sitting at a café with your iPad, follow this loop to ensure you don't lose work:

Action	Command Button	Why?
Start	Pull (Blue arrow down)	Gets the latest changes you made on your Mac.
Work	Write your code	Do your analysis/data science.
Save	Commit	Checkmarks the files you changed and adds a note.
Finish	Push (Green arrow up)	Sends your iPad work back to GitHub.
Crucial Setup for iPad Users
To avoid typing your 40-character GitHub token every time you want to save
work on your iPad, run this command once in the Posit Cloud console:

R
install.packages("gitcreds")
gitcreds::gitcreds_set()
Paste your GitHub Personal Access Token when prompted. Now, Posit Cloud will remember you, and "Pushing" will be a one-click process.

A Final Warning on Data
GitHub is great for code, but it has a 100MB limit per file. If your project involves massive .csv or
.rds data files, don't upload them to GitHub. Instead, keep the data in Posit Cloud's local storage or a cloud bucket.

Would you like me to explain how to generate a GitHub Personal Access Token (PAT) if you don't have one yet?

  This is a vital step because once you push a file to GitHub, it is very difficult to "un-see" it, even
  if you delete it later. This is especially true if your project contains passwords, API keys, or private spreadsheets.

The tool we use for this is a simple text file called .gitignore.

1. What is a .gitignore?
  It is a "burn list" for Git. Any file or folder name listed inside this file will be completely ignored by
  GitHub. It will stay on your Mac and on Posit Cloud, but it will never be uploaded to the internet.

2. How to create/edit it
In your RStudio console (on your Mac or Posit Cloud), run:

  R
usethis::edit_git_ignore()
This will open a text file in your editor. You should add the following types of files:

  Data files: .csv, .xlsx, .rds, .sqlite

Large PDFs: Since you mentioned Folder1 has PDFs, you might want to exclude them if they are large or private.

Environment files: .Renviron (this is where we usually hide secret keys).

Your .gitignore should look something like this:

  Plaintext
.Rhistory
.RData
.Rproj.user/
  *.csv
*.xlsx
*.rds
private_folder/
  3. How to handle "Secrets" (API Keys)
If your code needs a password to access a database or an API, do not type password
<- "my_secret_123" in your script. Instead:

  Run usethis::edit_r_environ().

Type your secret like this: MY_SECRET_KEY=123456789.

Save and restart R.

In your script, use: key <- Sys.getenv("MY_SECRET_KEY").

Ensure .Renviron is listed in your .gitignore file.

Summary Checklist for your Holiday Trip
Before you zip up your suitcase and head out, do a quick "Dry Run":

  Step	Action	Check
1	Commit & Push from your Mac.	[ ]
2	Open Posit Cloud on your iPad.	[ ]
3	Pull the changes.	[ ]
4	Check .gitignore: Ensure your private data isn't showing up in the Git pane.	[ ]
A Quick Tip for the iPad
If you find the iPad screen too small for the 4-pane RStudio layout, you can
minimize panes by clicking the small window icons in the top right of each quadrant. This gives you much more room to actually see your code.

Would you like me to explain how to set up a "Project" in Posit Cloud specifically
so it doesn't time out while you're mid-code on the beach?

Coding on an iPad is a unique experience. Because iPadOS aggressively manages background power,
it can occasionally "kill" your connection to Posit Cloud if you look away for too long.

Here is how to set up your project—and your iPad—to prevent those annoying mid-code timeouts.

1. Adjust iPad "Auto-Lock" Settings
The biggest culprit for a disconnected R session isn't Posit Cloud; it’s the iPad itself.
When the screen turns off, the browser pauses your connection.

Go to Settings > Display & Brightness > Auto-Lock.

Change this to 15 Minutes or Never while you are working.

Note: If you don't see "Never," it might be because your iPad has a work/email profile
(MDM) installed that restricts it for security.

2. Configure Posit Cloud Project Settings
Posit Cloud has its own "Idle Timeout." On the free tier, this is usually set to 1 hour.
This means if you don't interact with the IDE for 60 minutes, the session suspends.

Open your project in Posit Cloud.

Click the Settings (gear icon) in the top right.

Go to the Resources tab.

Check the Idle Timeout. While you can't always increase this on the Free tier,
knowing the limit helps. If you step away for lunch, just remember to save your work and Push to GitHub first.

3. Use "Background Jobs" for Long Scripts
If you are running a script that takes 10 minutes to finish (like a complex model or a large data scrape),
don't run it in the main console. On an iPad, if the screen dims during a console run, the process might stop.

Instead, use the Background Jobs tab:

  In the "Jobs" pane (usually near the Console), click Start Background Job.

Select your script.

Even if your iPad screen flickers or you switch tabs to check an
email, Posit Cloud will keep running that job on its servers.

4. Handling the "Session Suspended" Message
On your holiday, you might open your iPad and see: "While you were away, your project went to sleep."

Don't panic.

Posit Cloud is excellent at "hibernating." Usually, clicking Relaunch or refreshing
the page will bring back your exact environment, including unsaved code in your scripts.

However, unsaved variables in your Environment (top right) can sometimes be lost.
This is why you should always end your sessions by clicking Push in the Git pane.

The "Beach Workflow" Recap
Pull your code (gets your Mac's work).

Code (with iPad Auto-Lock set to a high duration).

Commit & Push (saves your work to the "cloud within the cloud").

Would you like me to show you how to customize the RStudio "look" (like a Dark Mode)
so it’s easier to see your code if you’re working outside in bright sunlight?

  Visual comfort is a game-changer when you're transitioning from a dim indoor office to
  a bright holiday environment. Since you’re on a MacBook M1 and an iPad, you’re likely
  used to high-quality Retina displays, which make these adjustments look great.1.
  High-Contrast Themes for SunlightIf you are working outside or near a window,
  a Dark Mode might actually be harder to read due to screen glare.
  You want a high-contrast Light Theme.Go to: Tools > Global Options...
  > Appearance.Editor Theme: * For Outdoors: Try "Textmate (default)" or "Chrome".
  These provide a clean white background that fights glare.For Night/Indoors:
  Try "Tomorrow Night 80s" or "Merbivore". They are easy on the eyes in low light.2.
  Zoom and UI ScalingThe iPad browser can sometimes make the RStudio buttons look tiny.
  Instead of pinching the screen (which can mess up the cursor alignment), use RStudio’s
  internal scaling:In the same Appearance menu, look for Zoom.Set it to 110% or 120%. This makes the
  tabs and icons much easier to tap with a finger if you aren't using a trackpad.3. The "Completeness" Checklist:
  Final SummarySince we've covered a lot, here is your definitive "Mac-to-iPad-to-Mac" workflow to
  ensure you never lose a line of code:StageActionToolSetupAdd data/secrets to
  .gitignore.usethis::edit_git_ignore()Sync (Out)Commit and Push all changes from
  MacBook.Git Pane (Green Arrow)TravelLog into Posit Cloud on iPad Safari/Chrome.posit.cloudSync
  (In)Pull changes before you type a single word.Git Pane (Blue Arrow)WorkUse Background Jobs for
  heavy lifting.Jobs PaneSaveCommit & Push before closing the iPad lid.Git PaneOne Last Hardware TipThe
  MacBook M1 and the iPad Pro both use USB-C. If you are traveling, you only need one charger
  for both. However, if your iPad starts to lag while using Posit Cloud, check your battery.
  iPadOS often throttles browser performance when the battery drops below 10%.Since you're
  all set for your trip, would you like me to show you how to create a "ReadMe"
  file for your project so you remember exactly what those PDF files in Folder1 were for when you get back?

  A README.md file is the "front door" of your project. Since you mentioned Folder1
  contains PDFs and information not directly in the R project, a README is
  the perfect place to document what those files are so you don't have to
  go hunting through folders when you return from holiday.

1. Create the README
In your RStudio console (on Mac or Posit Cloud), run:

R
usethis::use_readme_md()
This creates a blank Markdown file and opens it.

2. What to Include for "Future You"
Since you are balancing two locations (Mac and Posit Cloud), use this structure to stay organized:

Markdown
# Project Name: [Folder2 Name]

## 📍 Project Locations
* **Local:** `Dropbox/Folder1/Folder2` (Contains raw PDFs and source docs)
* **Cloud:** Posit Cloud Instance [Link here]

## 📂 Folder1 Contents (Reference Only)
* `Manual_v1.pdf`: Documentation for the data source.
* `Research_Paper.pdf`: The theory behind this analysis.

## 🛠 Setup Notes
* Required Packages: `tidyverse`, `ggplot2`, `gitcreds`.
* **Data Note:** Large CSVs are ignored by Git; manually upload to Posit Cloud if missing.

## 🗓 Holiday Progress
* [ ] Task 1
* [ ] Task 2
3. Seeing the "Rendered" Version
One of the best parts of RStudio is the Preview button.

Click the Preview button at the top of the README.md editor.

It will show you a nicely formatted version of your notes.

When you Commit and Push this file, it will also appear as a beautiful, readable webpage on your GitHub repository's main page.

Final "Peace of Mind" Tip
Since you are using Dropbox on your Mac but GitHub for the iPad/Cloud sync:

  Conflict Warning: Avoid having your R project open on your Mac while you are editing it on your iPad.

Dropbox and Git can sometimes fight over file locks.

The Golden Rule: Close RStudio on your Mac before you head out the door.

You're ready for your trip! ✈️
You have a synced workflow, a secure way to handle secrets, a "glare-proof" screen setup, and documentation to keep you on track.

Is there anything else—perhaps a specific R package or a data visualization trick—you'd like to brush up on before you head out?
